content.text: Every architectural decision for an AI system carries a hidden cost: the cost of undoing it. Some decisions — like choosing your embedding model — lock in so much downstream work that reversing them requires months of re-indexing, re-evaluation, and re-tuning. Others — like the wording of a system prompt — can be reversed in minutes. Understanding this spectrum before making a decision is what separates teams that stay agile from teams that box themselves in.

Architecture decision reversibility is the degree to which a made decision can be undone without disproportionate cost. Amazon popularized the Type 1 / Type 2 framework: Type 1 decisions are one-way doors (hard or impossible to reverse), and Type 2 decisions are two-way doors (easy to revisit). The goal is to identify which AI architecture decisions fall into which category before committing — not after.

In the AI context, reversibility depends on three factors: migration cost (compute and engineering time to undo), data loss risk (whether reversing destroys information that cannot be reconstructed), and downtime required (whether undoing the decision needs a service outage).

the field moves faster than any other software domain. A decision that looks optimal today may be superseded by a better model or vector database next quarter. Reversibility determines how painful that transition is.
Irreversible decisions accumulate technical debt silently:

each one narrows the option space for future decisions. Teams that make several irreversible decisions in sequence can find themselves locked into an architecture that cannot be improved without a complete rewrite.
without a framework, teams treat all decisions as equally weighty. A scoring rubric forces the team to quantify the reversibility of each option before choosing.
Preserves optionality as the AI landscape evolves. You can swap a reversible component without disrupting the rest of the system.
Reduces incident blast radius. When a reversible decision proves wrong in production, the rollback is measured in minutes rather than weeks.
Makes experimentation safer. Teams are more willing to try novel retrieval strategies or prompt architectures if they know those decisions are reversible.
Forces abstraction discipline. To make a decision reversible, you must hide it behind an interface — which also improves testability and replaceability.

Reversibility costs code complexity. Abstraction layers that allow swapping vector databases add indirection. This is worth it for irreversible-by-nature decisions but overkill for trivially reversible ones.

Not all irreversibility is bad. Sometimes a specific, tightly coupled choice delivers the best performance. The goal is to make the decision consciously, not to maximize reversibility for its own sake.
Reversibility scoring adds process overhead. Teams must assess every significant architectural decision. For fast-moving teams, this can feel like a tax — keep the process lightweight.

Think of architectural decisions as investments with different liquidity profiles. A reversible decision is a liquid investment — you can exit it at low cost if the market changes. An irreversible decision is an illiquid investment — it may pay off well, but you cannot easily exit if circumstances change. The reversibility score is the liquidity rating of each architectural choice.
Before making a Score 1-2 decision, ask: what would it take to undo this in 12 months? If the answer is painful, invest more time in evaluation before committing.
Score each AI architecture decision on a 1-5 scale based on the worst-case cost of undoing it:
Months of eng time + massive compute
Weeks of eng time + significant compute
Minutes — git revert or config change

attrs.alt: Reversibility spectrum from irreversible (embedding model, vector DB schema) to trivially reversible (prompt templates, model provider) with ADR template fields

content.text: the embedding model determines the dimensionality and semantic space of every vector in your store. Switching models (e.g., from OpenAI text-embedding-3-small to Cohere embed-v3) requires re-embedding the entire corpus, rebuilding all indices, re-evaluating retrieval quality, and retraining any components that depend on the embedding space.

the namespace structure, field definitions, and metadata schema of a vector store are costly to change. Adding a new mandatory field requires a full re-index. Renaming a namespace requires migrating all data and updating all callers.

the data model for user long-term memory (episodic memory, preference stores, conversation summaries). A schema change that alters how memories are structured may render existing memories unreadable by the new schema.
the token format, session ID scheme, and permission model for agents. Changing these invalidates all active sessions and integration contracts with external systems.
system prompts, few-shot examples, and output format instructions. Stored in version control, deployable via config update, rollbackable with git revert. Score 5.
switching between providers (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) is Score 4 if you maintain a provider abstraction layer. Without abstraction, it drops to Score 2.
switching from dense-only to hybrid (dense + BM25) retrieval is a query-path change that does not require re-indexing existing vectors. Score 4.
which tools an agent can call. Register or deregister tools at runtime with no data migration. Score 5.
pinning to claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022 vs claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219 is a config string change. Score 5.

An Architecture Decision Record (ADR) captures a decision, its context, and its consequences. The AI-specific template adds a reversibility score and an alternatives section that explicitly considers reversibility:

# ADR-0042: Embedding Model Selection

## Status
Accepted — 2026-03-15

## Context
We need an embedding model for our RAG system. The corpus is 500k product documents
in English. Retrieval quality and cost per query are the primary constraints.

## Decision
Use text-embedding-3-large (OpenAI) at 3072 dimensions.

## Reversibility Score
**Score: 1 — Irreversible**
- Migration cost: re-embed 500k documents (~$200 compute + 2 weeks eng time)
- Data loss risk: none (source documents retained), but all downstream eval baselines
  are invalidated on model switch
- Downtime: rolling re-index required (estimated 48h for full corpus)
- Approval required: VP Engineering sign-off

## Alternatives Considered
| Option                 | Rev. Score | Retrieval Quality | Cost/1k tokens |
|------------------------|-----------|-------------------|----------------|
| text-embedding-3-large |     1     | Best (MTEB 64.6)  | $0.13          |
| text-embedding-3-small |     1     | Good (MTEB 62.3)  | $0.02          |
| Cohere embed-v3        |     1     | Good (MTEB 64.5)  | $0.10          |

All options score 1 — embedding model choice is inherently irreversible.
text-embedding-3-large selected for quality given corpus size.

## Consequences
- All future model switches require full re-index and eval re-baseline
- Lock in to OpenAI embedding API — add abstraction layer to allow future migration
- Dimension count (3072) must be reflected in vector store schema (also Score 1)

## Owner
platform-team · Expires for review: 2027-03-15
Decision Trees for Common AI Architecture Choices
When should you build an abstraction layer? The rule: build abstraction when the underlying decision is Score 1-2. Skip it when the decision is Score 4-5.
interface that wraps the provider SDK. If the choice is Score 2 (tight coupling to provider features), the abstraction pays off quickly.
methods. Accept that the schema is still locked to the specific store's capabilities, but at least the calling code is portable.
no abstraction needed. Store prompts in YAML or Markdown files in version control. Use a simple load function.

# Provider abstraction layer — makes LLM provider a Score 4 decision
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class LLMResponse:
    content: str
    input_tokens: int
    output_tokens: int
    model: str

class LLMClient(ABC):
    """Abstraction over LLM providers. Swap provider by changing implementation."""

    @abstractmethod
    async def complete(self, system: str, user: str, max_tokens: int = 1000) -> LLMResponse:
        pass

class AnthropicClient(LLMClient):
    def __init__(self, model: str = "claude-sonnet-4-5"):
        import anthropic
        self._client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()
        self._model = model

    async def complete(self, system: str, user: str, max_tokens: int = 1000) -> LLMResponse:
        resp = await self._client.messages.create(
            model=self._model,
            max_tokens=max_tokens,
            system=system,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user}],
        )
        return LLMResponse(
            content=resp.content[0].text,
            input_tokens=resp.usage.input_tokens,
            output_tokens=resp.usage.output_tokens,
            model=resp.model,
        )

# To switch providers: change one line
# llm = AnthropicClient()  →  llm = OpenAIClient("gpt-4o")
# All calling code is unaffected
Before choosing any component that stores data (vector store, memory DB, session store). These are almost always Score 1-2.
Before selecting an embedding model for a production RAG system. Score 1 by definition.
Before designing the memory schema for a long-lived agent. Score 1-2 depending on data volume.
At the start of any significant AI system refactor, to identify which existing decisions are already locked in vs which can be changed freely.
Run a reversibility review at the start of each new major feature or architecture change. The process takes 30-60 minutes:
List every architectural decision implied by the change (embedding model, schema, provider, retrieval strategy, etc.).
Score each decision 1-5 using the rubric above. Decisions scoring 1-2 require a written ADR with the reversibility score, alternatives, and sign-off level.
For each Score 1-2 decision, build an abstraction layer or document explicitly why abstraction is not feasible.
and link it from the pull request.
Treating all decisions as equally important:
spending the same due diligence on prompt wording (Score 5) as on embedding model selection (Score 1) is poor allocation of review time.
Skipping the ADR for Score 1 decisions because the team is in a hurry:
the decisions made in a hurry are precisely the ones that come back most expensively. Score 1 decisions require more review time, not less.
interface does not make the embedding schema reversible. It makes the calling code portable. The data itself is still locked in.
ADRs filed and never revisited become stale. Set an explicit expiry date on each ADR and schedule a review when the AI landscape changes significantly.
Score reversibility before the decision, not after. Once a vector store is populated with 10M embeddings, the reversibility discussion is moot.
Set a sign-off level by score. Score 1-2 decisions require VP Engineering approval. Score 3 requires team lead. Score 4-5 decisions need only the author's judgment.
Include reversibility score in all architecture diagrams and pull request descriptions.
Prefer Score 4-5 alternatives when they are within 10% of the performance of a Score 1-2 alternative. Pay for reversibility with small performance concessions.
adr-tools (CLI for managing ADR files in git), log4brains (web UI for browsing ADRs).
LiteLLM provides a unified interface over 100+ LLM providers — makes provider choice Score 4.
LangChain's VectorStore class, LlamaIndex's VectorStoreIndex — note that these abstract the API, not the schema or data.
Score 1-2 decisions often also have the largest security surface. Changing the embedding model or memory store means auditing access controls in the new system from scratch.
Irreversible data schema decisions lock in the encryption scheme. If PII is stored in the memory store without field-level encryption, adding it later may require a full schema migration.
ADRs for Score 1-2 decisions should explicitly document the data retention and residency implications of the chosen option.
Type 1 (irreversible) decisions for AI systems include embedding model choice, vector DB schema, and memory store schema — they require months and significant compute to undo.
Type 2 (reversible) decisions include prompt templates, model provider, retrieval strategy, and tool selection — these can be changed in hours or minutes.
The reversibility rubric scores decisions 1-5 based on migration cost, data loss risk, and downtime required. Score 1-2 require VP sign-off; Score 4-5 need only team judgment.
Write an ADR for every Score 1-2 decision. Include context, decision, reversibility score, alternatives with their scores, and consequences.
Build abstraction layers for Score 1-2 decisions to at least make the calling code portable, even if the data itself is locked in.
Score reversibility before the decision. Once a 10M-vector corpus exists, the discussion is academic.
Prefer Score 4-5 options when they are within 10% of the performance of a Score 1-2 option — pay for reversibility with small performance trade-offs.
